segments.text: These 144 pixels started a language. In 1999, designer Shing Taka Kurita built
176 symbols on a strict 12x 12 grid for Japanese phones. Sun, heart, car, simple picture that carried feelings. Those little pictures spread everywhere.
Today, billions of emojis move across phones every single day, turning quick taps into clear emotion. Your keyboard still speak Japanese. Ramen, Naruto,
fishcake, sake, even local science, a global language born in Tokyo. Emojis literally mean picture character. And
Kurita's original 176 are now in the modern arts museum. Prove that constraints can even create some amazing things. If you want more amazing design stories, subscribe.
